coredata.dc:title: Potato peel carbon dots produced via fixed bed pyrolysis reactor decorated 1-D TiO2 nanorods for rapid removal of methylene blue, Cr (VI), Escherichia coli, and Aspergillus niger

coredata.dc:description: In this work, new type carbon quantum dots produced from waste of biomass were used as a dopant molecule in order to investigated photocatalytic properties of TiO2 nanorods structures. Low-cost and eco-friendly carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were obtained via slow pyrolysis of potato peels (PP) as a biomass waste without any chemicals by fixed bed pyrolysis. Potato peel quantum dots (P-CQDs) decorated TiO2 nanorods (P-CQDs/TNRs) were synthesized were by utilizing their high specific surface area, good dispersion, abundant surface functional groups. (P-CQDs) were imposed on the surface of the one-dimensional TiO2 nanorods fabricated via chemical impregnation method for P-CQDs/TiO2 nanorods (P-CQDs/TNRs) that TiO2 nanorods have been fabricated by hydrothermal route. Characteristic properties of as-prepared P-CQDs/TNRs photocatalysts were characterized employing TGA, XRD, XPS, FE-SEM/EDS, HRTEM, FTIR, fluorescence and UV–vis spectroscopy, resp. The photocatalytic activity of as-prepared P-CQDs/TNRs were investigated for methylene blue, hexavalent chromium, Escherichia coli and Aspergillus niger under UV-A. The obtained results suggest that the photocatalytic activity of P-CQDs/TNRs composites was slightly enhanced as compared to that of TNRs. The scenario behind the superior photocatalytic activity of P-CQDs/TNRs may be having a synergetic effect between P-CQDs molecules and the surface of TNRs, and also the design of the charge-transfer channel of TNRs. Moreover, the carbon derived from a natural green source possesses the various functional groups such as CC, C-OH, C-O-C, CO, and when these functional groups deposited to the surface of the TiO2 nanorods, newly formed nanocomposites can improve the visible light-driven photocatalytic activity.

head.ce:title: Potato peel carbon dots produced via fixed bed pyrolysis reactor decorated 1-D TiO2 nanorods for rapid removal of methylene blue, Cr (VI), Escherichia coli, and Aspergillus niger

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, new type carbon quantum dots produced from waste of biomass were used as a dopant molecule in order to investigated photocatalytic properties of TiO2 nanorods structures. Low-cost and eco-friendly carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were obtained via slow pyrolysis of potato peels (PP) as a biomass waste without any chemicals by fixed bed pyrolysis. Potato peel quantum dots (P-CQDs) decorated TiO2 nanorods (P-CQDs/TNRs) were synthesized were by utilizing their high specific surface area, good dispersion, abundant surface functional groups. (P-CQDs) were imposed on the surface of the one-dimensional TiO2 nanorods fabricated via chemical impregnation method for P-CQDs/TiO2 nanorods (P-CQDs/TNRs) that TiO2 nanorods have been fabricated by hydrothermal route. Characteristic properties of as-prepared P-CQDs/TNRs photocatalysts were characterized employing TGA, XRD, XPS, FE-SEM/EDS, HRTEM, FTIR, fluorescence and UV–vis spectroscopy, resp. The photocatalytic activity of as-prepared P-CQDs/TNRs were investigated for methylene blue, hexavalent chromium, Escherichia coli and Aspergillus niger under UV-A. The obtained results suggest that the photocatalytic activity of P-CQDs/TNRs composites was slightly enhanced as compared to that of TNRs. The scenario behind the superior photocatalytic activity of P-CQDs/TNRs may be having a synergetic effect between P-CQDs molecules and the surface of TNRs, and also the design of the charge-transfer channel of TNRs. Moreover, the carbon derived from a natural green source possesses the various functional groups such as CC, C-OH, C-O-C, CO, and when these functional groups deposited to the surface of the TiO2 nanorods, newly formed nanocomposites can improve the visible light-driven photocatalytic activity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The presence of persistent contaminants such as toxic organic and inorganic materials or hazardous living microorganisms in aquatic environments gives rise to both severe threats to water quality and community health (Tong et al., 2022). Deterioration in the water sources has dramatically gone up due to the rapid development of science, and industrial technology, listless exploitation of groundwater, and human activity, hindering access to safe drinking water (Bui et al., 2022; Shen et al., 2022). Different from the existing conventional methods including biological, physical, and chemical, photocatalysis is considered as a green and eco-friendly process in order to eliminate various toxic substances from wastewater (Farjadfar et al., 2022). Of great interest, with this novel approach, inorganic materials can be photo-reduction and oxidation, organic substances can be degraded, and also proliferation of different microorganisms can be inhibited.

Among the numerous semiconductor families (e.g., CdS, Bi2S3, GaP, Fe2O3, TiO2, ZnO, WO3, BiVO4, V2O5, Ag2O, SrTiO3, Ta3N, Al2O3, and C3N4) utilized in photocatalysis researches so as to eliminate toxic substances, TiO2 is acknowledged as one of the most suitable and promising candidates because of its qualities, which include low toxicity, high chemical stability, excellent oxidation properties, high electron mobility, and low cost overall (Bui et al., 2022; Roza et al., 2020; Kumar et al., 2018; Feng et al., 2020; Liang et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2014). However, being its too large band gaps (e.g., ∼3.0 and ∼3.2 eV for rutile and anatase phases, respectively), TiO2 gives both responses to visible light in the range of UV and fast recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs (
e
−
/
h
+
), which pave the way of hindering the application of TiO2 nanostructures in photocatalysis (Liang et al., 2019; Bian et al., 2014). In an attempt to overcome such drawbacks, it has been improved a number of strategies comprising some strategies such as ion introduction, coupled with other semiconductors or materials sensitization, metal and nonmetal deposition, and dye sensitization (Shen et al., 2022). Hereby, these alternatives might prolong the absorption spectrum in the visible light range and effectively facilitate high separation by minimizing the recombination of the photogenerated (
e
−
/
h
+
) pair (Ding et al., 2022; Xie et al., 2023).

In recent years, as a new class of environmentally friendly nanomaterials, the decorated quantum dots (CQDs) with quasi-zero or zero-dimensional to enhance photogenerated electron transfer have attracted considerable attention owing to outstanding characteristics, such as chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility, high aqueous solubility, unpeered up-converted photoluminescence (PL) property, low toxicity, electron reservoir, and transporter behaviors (Sun et al., 2023; Su et al., 2020; Mandal et al., 2021), which indicate that CQDs can act as a co-catalyst in draft of new kind of photocatalyst. CQDs with diameters smaller than 10 nm, a unique class of carbon nanomaterials, contain a large amount of surface functional groups including -OH, -COOH and -NH2, which are attribute to photoluminescent qualities of CQDs. In addition, these functional groups not only promote water solubility, the separation, and transmission of photogenerated carriers but also take an active charge in functionalization of CQDs (Farjadfar et al., 2022; Su et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2022). Since the large grapheme-like conjugated planes of CQDs expedite a considerable extent the separation and transmission of photogenerated carriers, it has drawn attention in application areas, such as photoelectronics, biotechnology, chemical and biological sensing, and photocatalysis (Shen et al., 2022).

There are many studies that used CQDs as co-catalysts to improve the various new types of semiconductors-based materials. In this regard, Liu et al. (2017) synthesized TiO2/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) composites with different CQDs contents via solvothermal and in situ hydrothermal methods for photodegradation of rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation (Liu et al., 2017), while Tong and co-authors reported titanium dioxide-coated carbon quantum dots (CQDs/TiO2) based composite prepared by sol-gel hydrolysis for photodegradation of rhodamine B (RhB) in aqueous solution (Tong et al., 2022). In both studies, it has been stated that photocatalytic degradation efficiency of RhB increased because CQDs behaved charge separation centers by inhibiting the recombination of electron/hole pairs and developed the surface area TiO2 particles. A new CQDs/ZnO2 photocatalyst system was produced by El-Shamy (2020) by exploiting up-conversion luminescence and photoinduced electron transfer behavior of CQDs that this novel composites were showed higher photocatalytic removal in the degradation of methyl orange, methylene blue, and rhodamine B dyes due to synergetic effect and the design of charge-transfer channel (El-Shamy, 2020). Shen et al. (2022) and Yu et al. (2014) deposited carbon quantum onto the TiO2 nanosheets (TNs) dots to enhance performance in the decomposition of congo red and rhodamine B under visible light that the introduction of CDs onto TiO2 nanosheets can extent to visible light absorption associated with generation of more electron/hole pairs and unmatched unique up-converted photoluminescence behavior of CQDs for an enhanced degradation performance (Shen et al., 2022; Yu et al., 2014). Another study found that CDs bounded Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower composites regulated electron transferring between HOMO and LUMO, increasing the photo-removal of phenol, norfloxacin, tetrabromobisphenol A and Cr (VI) pollutants (Chen et al., 2022). Ding and co-worker have hybridized In2S3/TiO2 heterojunction with CQDs to promote the separation of photogenerated (
e
−
/
h
+
) pair pairs and maintaining high redox capacity, and also investigated both photoelectrochemical properties and degradation of ofloxacin. It has been stated that in situ synthesized CQD/TiO2 nanostructures created by Sathish Kumar et al. (2018) showed higher degradation rate owing to radicals formed in consequence of interfacial charge transfer from the TiO2 to CQD molecules (Kumar et al., 2018). Similarly, Zhou et al. (2019) has reported that simple production of carbon quantum dot (CQD)-sensitized TiO2/Pt nanocomposites facilitated charge transfer from CQDs to TiO2, ultimately improving TiO2’s performance for light-driven H2 generation (Zhou et al., 2019). It has been determined that CQDs inside a new kind of composite (LM-TiO2) used to stimulate methyl orange as the model pollutant in wastewater can substantially support to the light absorption intensity and broaden the absorption range photocatalytic performance of TiO2 (Zhang et al., 2021). In literature research, ZnO@CQDs nanocomposites produced with modification of CQDs were used to test antibacterial activity, and then induce the separation rate of
e
−
/
h
+
pairs under visible light irradiation, leading strong antimicrobial activity by generating more the reactive oxygen species (Gao et al., 2020). Safardoust-Hojaghan et al. (2021) designed CQDs synthesized from biomass (soya chunk) as a carbon source decorated ZnO nanoparticles, and they found that CQDs-surface covering of ZnO NPs and lead to the cease of the inhibitory effect of ZnO NPs (Safardoust-Hojaghan et al., 2021). It has been showed that functional groups found on the CQDs, which derived from different biomass were enhanced the performance of solid-state nanostructure solar cells (Briscoe et al., 2015). Zirak and co-authors have stated that the introduction of CQDs inside the ZnO matrix contributed to creating the super-hydrophilic surface of ZnO thin films (Zirak et al., 2021). Based on the researches given above, anchoring CQDs with zero-dimensional on the heterojunction structure can be one of the notable modification methods to achieve high photocatalytic performance.

Highly ordered one-dimensional TNRs aligned vertically, which exhibit unique properties such as short diffusion length for photogenerated (
e
−
/
h
+
) pairs, and enhanced light harvesting compared to spherical morphology (Mahammed Shaheer et al., 2021), were successfully synthesized by direct hydrothermal process. By taking advantage of the peerless properties of CQDs, potato peels–a domestic waste- as raw material precursors were carbonized at low temperatures via a fix-bed pyrolysis reactor to produce the biomass-derived eco-friendly P-CQDs co-catalysts in the novelty of this study. A new high-performance hybrid system of P-CQDs was prepared by a one-step chemical impregnation method. It is anticipated that this composite hetero-structured semiconductor photocatalyst formed with decorating of P-CQDs to TiO2 nanorods would be able to enhance the light-harvesting, charge separation, and charge transport. The photocatalytic activity and photokilling of P-CQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites was evaluated by the removal of Cr (VI), degradation of MB, and inactivation of Escherichia coli (E-coli) and Aspergillus niger (A. niger) microorganisms under visible light irradiation.

PP were provided from Yeşilbük Village, Trabzon in Turkey. 1,5-Diphenyl Carbazide (C13H14ON4), as the complexing agent, was purchased from LaboChemie. Commercial TiO2 (anatase) nanoparticles (≥99 %), methylene blue, acetone, sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥99 %) were supplied by Sigma Aldrich. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 %) was obtained from Carlo Erba Company. Potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7, ≥99.9 %) was obtained from AppliChem (Panreac ITW Companies). Irradiation source equipped with a 6 W (X2) dual lamp, emitting radiation at 254/365 nm (light intensity 350 mW/cm2) was provided from Spectro line ENF260. Müeller Hinton Agar (MHB) and Sabaroud Dextroz Agar (SDA) were purchased from Merck Millipore. Phosphate buffer (PBS) saline was supplied from Pan Biotech firm. In the further purification of P-CQDs was used Serva brand as a dialysis bag. E. coli (ATCC 25922) and isolates of A. niger were procured from the Karadeniz Technical University, Department of Medical Microbiology and Hacettepe University, Department of Medical Microbiology, Mycology laboratory. Deionized water was supplied by a Merck Millipore Direct-Q® 8 UV. Ultrasonic bath is used Bandelin Sonorex Digitec. All reagents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. Fresh distilled water (DW) was used throughout all tests, experiments, treatments, and rinsing.

The as-prepared photocatalysts were characterized by TGA, XRD, Uv–vis absorbance spectroscopy, XPS, FE-SEM/EDS, HRTEM, FTIR, fluorescence spectroscopy, and UV–vis DRS analyses. FTIR spectra of the photocomposites was conducted on a Perkin Elmer 1600 FTIR spectrometer by scanning from 400–400 cm−1 that connected with integrated ATR. The optical properties of P-CQDs/TiO2 photocatalysts were recorded by UV-Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV 3600 Plus) in which BaSO4 was used as the reflectance standard material. The crystal structure of as-prepared photocatalysts was acquired on a PANalytical Empyrean XRD using a diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ=0⊡154056 nm) (60 kV, 100 mA) over an angular range from 10°-90°. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were measured by Specs-Flex XPS with monochromated Al Kα radiation to analyze the surface oxidation states of the element in the P-CQDs/TiO2 NRs and pristine TiO2 NRs. All fluorescence spectra of P-CQDs liquid samples were recorded on a Photon Technologies International Quanta Master-Spectrofluorimeter (model QM-4/2006) spectrofluorometer using a slit of 5 nm, with a photomultiplier tube voltage of 400 V and scanning speed of 1200 nm/min at room temperature. To examine details of the surface morphology of the samples, HRTEM and FE-SEM along with EDS images were observed using FEI TALOS F200S TEM (200 kV) and FEI Quanta 650 field emission SEM operated an accelerating voltage of 100 V-30 kV, respectively (Resolution: 1.2 nm@30 kV (SED)/0.8 nm@30 kV (STEM)), and EDS images were obtained using an Inca 250 SSD XMax20 detector which is Peltier cooled with 20 mm2 active area and 129 eV resolution used for elemental analysis. The pyrolysis characteristics of the PP were conceded using an Exstar SII TG/DTA 3600 instrument under a nitrogen atmosphere in the heating temperature from room temperature to 600 °C, the heating rate at 8 °C/min, and the flow rate of the carrier gas was fixed at 50 mL/min (particle size of PP powders: 200 mesh, pan: alumina). T carbonization experiment of PP powders was performed with a fix-bed pyrolysis reactor. PP was carbonized in a pyrolysis reactor at 282 °C at a constant heating rate of 5 °C/min under N2 atmosphere (biomass: 4 g). UV–visible absorption spectra were collected on a Analytik Jena/Specord 210 plus to both determine the optical properties of P-CQDs liquid samples and calculate the removal of MB and Cr (VI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pyrolysis of PP powders and eco-friendly synthesis of P-CQDs liquid solution

ce:list-item.ce:para: Prior to the preparation of the P-CQDs liquid solution, PP was washed with distilled water and dried in a hot air oven at 80 °C for 2 h. PPs were cut into small pieces using a sterilized knife and then well-grounded by laboratory-type pulverizing ring mill (Ünal Mühendislik, Turkey) to make fine powder. Produced PP powders were sieved up to 200 mesh (i.e. particle size ≤ 74 μm), and then it was stored for the pyrolysis step by drying at 100 °C for one night in an oven. The pyrolysis temperature of PP powders was confirmed by a thermogravimetric analyzer (TG/DTA). 5 mg of corresponding sample at particle size of 200 mesh loaded in alumina crucible was heated in range of 20–600 °C at constant heating rate of 8 °C/min by utilizing nitrogen as a carrier gas at a flow rate of 50 mL/min so as to ensure maximal efficiency temperature of pyrolysis reaction.

4 g of the PP powders were ejected into the tubular reactor without surface treatment under a nitrogen atmosphere for 2 h followed by an isothermal step in which pyrolysis temperature was selected at 282 °C (heating rate 5 °C/min), which is mid-point temperature verified experimentally by TG/DTG analysis. Nitrogen as a carrier gas has flowed into the pyrolysis system at the rate of 100 mL/min ( Fig. 1a). Afterward being cooled to room temperature, obtained dark black powders were stored in the dark. 0.5 g of obtained dark black powders from carbonized PP powders were ultrasonically dispersed in 50 mL of DW for 15 min (repeated 3 times). Dark black suspension was filtrated using different filter papers (S&H LabWare brand black (101), white (102), and blue (103), respectively) to remove the larger particles. Then, P-CQDs solution was collected by centrifuging at 100,000 rpm for 5 min to minor residues. At the end of the centrifugation, dark brown supernatant was filtered through a (ISOLAB) filtration membrane (0.45 μm) so as to stave off other insoluble impurities. For detailed purification, P-CQDs solution was dialyzed against the DW passed through regenerated cellulose structured a dialysis bag (MWCO: 6000–8000, diameter 28 mm) up to 48 h, and stored at 4 °C for further characterization and analytical studies. Images of P-CQDs solution under daylight and under UV-light at 365 nm are shown Fig. 1b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of TNRs and P-CQDs/TNRs composite

ce:list-item.ce:para: The hydrothermal process referred by (Ullas Krishnan and Jakka, 2022; Lafjah et al., 2014) was executed in the synthesis of TiO2 nanorod. 1 g of commercial TiO2 (anatase) powder was mixed with 80 mL of concentrated 10 M NaOH solution and sonicated in the ultrasonic bath for 30 min. Following dispersion, the mixture was infused into a 200 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with a lined PTFE and heated to 180 °C for 12 h in an oven. After the hydrothermal reaction, the reactor was naturally cooled down to ambient temperature followed by centrifuging at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove the excess NaOH solution. The obtained white precipitates were soaked many times in 0.1 M HCl solution for two day till approaching the neutral pH value (ca. 6.5–7.0) of the rinsing solution reached, and subsequently distilled water to detract from over ions. Finally, the drying step of precipitates was performed in air at 80 °C overnight in an oven, named as TNRs.

In order to decorate the P-CQDs on the surface of TNRs ( Fig. 2), it was used by a facile chemical impregnation process in which 0.1 g of TiO2 nanorods and a certain volume of P-CQDs (1, 5, 10, 15, 20, and 25 % of total volume) were dispersed by filling in 10 mL DW, and dispersed with ultrasonic for 30 min (at 15 min. intervals).

ce:section.ce:para: After vigorous stirring for 3 h at room temperature, the obtained pale yellow nanocomposites were dried under a vacuum at 80 °C until completely vaporized water in the sample for further characterization, photocatalytic, and photokilling experiments. Generated these composites with different volumes of P-CQDs were denoted as P-CQDs/TNRs-X in which ‘‘X’’ represents the added volume% of P-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation method of photocatalytic removal of MB and Cr (VI)
ce:section.ce:para:
In the photocatalytic removal experiments of MB and Cr (VI) as model pollutants, 0.1 g/L of P-CQDs/TNRs-X (X:1–5-10–15-20–25 % V) and naked TNRs were added in a cylindrical quartz reactor containing aqueous solution with a concentration of 10 mg/L MB. Irradiation of suspension was performed by stirring at 500 rpm with ultraviolet UV-A light placed at about 10 cm distance from the quartz cell for 120 min at ambient temperature. For the first 30 min, the photocatalytic reaction was carried out without irradiation to make sure of adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the photocatalyst and the pollutant solution in a dark media, after establishing equilibrium, the irradiation source was turned on. Throughout illumination, aliquots of 5 mL taken at certain time intervals were pipetted, and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to discriminate the photocatalysts from the pollutant solution. Photo-catalytically in the reduction from Cr (VI) to Cr (III), 10 mg/L Cr (VI) solution was prepared by dissolving potassium dichromate salt (K2Cr2O7) with DW. Subsequently, concentrated HCl was dropped, and adjusted the pH to 1.5. Next, 50 μL 10 mM of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) and 1 g/l of prepared of P-CQDs/TNRs-X were put in the Cr (VI) solution arranged pH value, and were magnetically stirred for 150 min. Photo-reduction experiments were initialized at similar conditions (light source, irradiation time, volume of aliquot, centrifugation step, etc.) that of MB degradation. Photo-reduction % efficiency of Cr (VI) in the supernatant was determined using the 1, 5-diphenylcarbazide colorimetric method using a UV–vis spectrometer (Wang et al., 2016; Rahmat et al., 2019). Photoremoval efficiency % of both MB and Cr (VI) during the photoreaction was recorded at a wavelength 664 and 540 nm nm employing UV–vis spectroscopy, respectively. The degradation efficiency % was determined by the following Eq. (1); (Tong et al., 2022; Farjadfar et al., 2022)
(1)

in which D stands for the degradation rate of MB and Cr (VI),

represents the concentration of pollutants at a certain time. ‘‘A’’ states the absorbance value, and also the definitions expressed for concentration are valid for absorbance equation. All experiments were repeated three times; the average value was taken.

In an attempt to scrutinize reusability tests after photocatalytic experiments, utilized P-CQDs/TNRs-X composites were taken away from supernatant solution by centrifuging at 10 000 rpm 15 min, and then washed with absolute ethanol, deionized H2O until MB or Cr (VI) from the composite surface were thoroughly desorbed, finally dried at 80 °C before the next test run.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photokilling measurements for E-coli and A. niger
ce:section.ce:para:
The photokilling behaviors as prepared photocatalysts were assessed in terms of the inactivation of Escherichia coli as a gram-negative bacteria and Aspergillus Niger standard strain as an indoor mold affecting the quality of water. 0.5 Mcfarland (
10
3
−
10
8
CFU / mL )
E. coli and A. niger were cultured in MHB agarose and SDA standards, respectively. Briefly, fresh liquid cultures of E. coli were prepared by inoculation in an MHB medium and incubation at 37 °C for 18–24 h under 150 rpm agitation on a shaker. To prepare the reaction suspensions, 1.0 mL of the liquid culture was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 15 min at 4 °C to recover the bacteria and washed with phosphate buffer (PBS) saline many times. Dilution transaction was applied to obtain until the optical density of the working concentration of bacteria suspension reached 0.5 McFarland unite at 600 nm. As for Aspergillus niger, fungal isolates of A. niger were rejuvenated using SDA media and incubated at 37 °C until sufficiently proliferated. Fungi spores with a dry cotton swap suspended in the desired volume of physiological saline solution were homogenized using a shaker with a speed of 100 rpm for 72 h. After the precipitated biomass was separated in fungal solution, the remaining transparent solution was filtrated using filter paper. Spore inoculum concentration was adjusted using the McFarland device for calculating the number of spores reaching 103-108 spore/mL (0.5 McFarland) for photokilling studies.

For the photokilling disinfection process, 1 g/L of the photocatalysts was added to 20 mL 0.5 McFarland of the E. coli suspension buffer and stirred at 500 rpm for 30 min before light irradiation (adsorption-desorption balance). 10 μL of aliquots withdrawn at the given time intervals (i.e. −30, 0, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150 min.) was impregnated onto the MHB agar plates and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h to form viable colony units. The working procedure for inhibiting the growth of fungi is similar to the procedure used for deactivation of E. coli cells. The only difference is that it was diluted 5000-fold and 1000-fold for E-coli and A. niger suspension, respectively before photocatalytic inactivation. Fungi spores were observed by incubating at 37 °C for 28 h, and it was photographed. It is worthy mentioning that all materials were autoclaved at 121 °C for 30 min before use, control experiments were carried out without the existence of nanoparticles, and finally experiments were applied three replications.
In order to evaluate the thermal characteristics of PPs, thermogravimetric analysis was carried out and the curve of TG/DTG analysis are illustrated in Fig. 3.

Considering the TG/DTG curve (Fig. 3), biomass has a very low thermal stability which decomposes at lower temperature regions (200–350 °C). The TG profile, which divides into two distinguishable weight losses. Firstly, small weight loss observed up to 115 °C could be associated with evaporation of the adsorbed water. Initially, the loss of biomass weight for temperatures lower than 115 °C was calculated at approximately 22 % due to the humidity loss. Typically, hemicellulose's thermal decomposition occurs between 150 and 350 °C, cellulose in the range 275–350 °C, and lignin between 250 and 500 °C (Chen et al., 2021; Chen and Kuo, 2010). Secondly, significant major weight loss is attributed to the thermal decomposition of hemicellulose and cellulose located at 120–350 °C, which agrees with the previous report by (Gao et al., 2022). In the TG analysis of the PPs, when the temperature reaches 350 °C, it was observed that a large loss of weight occurred at about 54.8 %, which corresponded to the hemicellulose and cellulose degradation, in tune with those of the data described by Santos et al., 2015 (2015). Based on the DTG curve, Magnacca et al. (2018) have reported that it is possible to designate an efficient pyrolysis temperature (Magnacca et al., 2018). At this point, it can be selected onset temperature (i.e., the initial temperature of material degradation) or the maximum rate of decomposition temperature (which corresponds to the maximum of the DTG curve). As seen in the DTG curve in Fig. 3, degradation temperature occurred maximum weight was selected 282 °C, which assigned midpoint temperature (the exothermic peak of DTG).

As illustrated in Fig. 4a, there was no observed absorbance in the 400 nm and up to the long wavelength in the UV–vis spectra of P-CQDs that diluted ten-fold. In contrast to the UV–vis area, absorption bands slightly occurred at around 287 and 320 nm, which indicated probably π-π* transition of CC bonds in aromatic sp2 hybridized carbon domain and n–π* transitions of the aromatic part possessing the CO/CN/O-H groups (Sariga et al., 2023; N et al., 2023). Whereas P-CQDs solution appears dark brown under daylight, it emits strong blue fluorescence when excited by a UV lamp (365 nm) (inset in Fig. 1b).

At the same time, the excitation wavelength-dependent emission properties of P-CQDs have been examined by fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 4b) as a function of excitation wavelengths from 300 to 400 nm. By varying the excitation wavelength values, the maximum intensity was observed with
λ
ex
350 nm with the emission peak at
λ
emi
41 nm. Fig. 4b presents the changes in fluorescence intensity of P-CQDs when excited by different wavelengths. When the aqueous solution of P-CQDs was excited at different wavelengths ranging from 300 to 350 nm, it was noticed that fluorescence intensities were increased, while fluorescence intensities were gradually decreased from 360 nm and up to 400 nm. In addition, the red shift was observed in the fluorescence spectra in the range 300–400 nm, wherein emission properties are impressed due to their size or diameter (Liao et al., 2023). This may be reason for excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior (Mathew et al., 2023; Sheng et al., 2021).
An X-ray diffraction (XRD) study has been carried out determine the crystalline nature of P-CQDs ( Fig. 5).

The XRD pattern displayed a single broad peak centered at 2θː23.6° due to the small-sized P-CQDs (Wang et al., 2022), indicating the specified (002) inter-layer lattice spacing of graphitic like-carbon-based materials (Shokri and Amjadi, 2022; Diao et al., 2018), and also obtained results are seemed quite in agreement with TEM data (Fig. 7). As indicated by Tong and co-workers (2022), the main characteristic XRD peaks of TNRs appear at 25.3°, 37.8°, 48.1°, 53.9°, 55.1°, 62.7°, 70.4° and 75.1° (JCPDS no. 21–1272), which all of these peaks can be indexed to the (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (116), (220), and (215) as facets of anatase TiO2 (Tong et al., 2022) ( Fig. 6).

Nevertheless, no specific peak at 23.6° for P-CQDs can be observed in the invaded TNRs samples with P-CQDs, suggesting that negligible effect on the crystal structure of TNRs. This phenomenon could be due to the low dose of P-CQDs in the P-CQDs/TNRs composites (Liu et al., 2017). Additionally, the obtained data revealed that the modification of P-CQDs into the TNRs dramatically affected the intensities of diffraction peaks of pristine TNRs, resulting in the lip spreading of specific peaks. This may be due to the fact that the deposition of P-CQDs critically disturbs the crystal structure of the TNRs (Tong et al., 2022; Shen et al., 2022), which may arrive the conclusion of the introduction of P-CQDs into the TNRs matrix. The crystallite size of TNRs nanoparticles and PCQDs/TNRs composites were calculated using Scherer’s formula (Safardoust-Hojaghan et al., 2021), and were found to be about 24.91 nm and 16.66 nm.

HRTEM analysis was performed to investigate the morphological and structure of P-CQDs, after it was evaporated aqueous phase of the dot solution, and put into ethanol solvent. It was calculated the average particle diameter of P-CQDs with ImageJ software by counting about 40 dots) and the particle size distribution was determined by fitting the Gaussian model. As depicted in Fig. 7a, the synthesized P-CQDs were agglomerated sphere-like shapes had a distribution with slightly agglomeration. P-CQDs exhibit a relatively narrow particle size distribution in the range of ranging from 1.2–2.8 nm. Additionally, the average particle diameter was estimated at ∼2.1±0.39 nm by scrutinizing the size of CQDs particles in the particle size histogram (Fig. 7b). Detailed HRTEM micrograph reveals that lattice spacing between planes is 0.32 nm, which indicates the (002) plane of the graphite sp2 structure, is in good accordance with results in literature (Rahal et al., 2021; Gan et al., 2022).

The surface functional groups of P-CQDs were characterized by FTIR. As shown in Fig. 8, a broad and intense band at 3323 cm−1 corresponds to the O–H/N-H stretching bands, which are attributed to rich in hydrophilic groups and amino-containing functional groups in the surface of the P-CQDs (Prathumsuwan et al., 2019). The peak at 2972 cm−1 was assigned to C-H stretching vibration. The minor peaks at 2882 cm−1 corresponded to the asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of the methylene group (Li et al., 2023). Characteristic peaks located at 1659 cm−1 and 1380 cm−1 appeared as the –CO/-CC stretching vibration and –C-H bending vibration, resp (Diao et al., 2018). The emerged peak around 1089/1048 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibrations of –C-O. The bands in low-frequency regions lower than 1048 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C-O groups of the different functional moieties (Başoğlu et al., 2020).

The functional groups of P-CQDs/TNRs composites were performed by FT-IR spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 9. On the subject of P-CQDs/TNRs, the absorption peaks at 3376 and 1637 cm−1 are marked to the stretching vibrations of the adsorbed water molecules and OH bonds. While the band at 755 cm−1 states to the out-of-plane bending vibration of H-Csp2, the absorption peak relevant to the TiOTi bond or skeletal vibration of symmetric carboxylate (–CO) stretching regarding P-CQDs occurred at 1380 cm−1 in P-CQDs/TNRs composite (Zhang et al., 2021). Further, absorption bands below 1000 cm−1 could arise from Ti-O, O-Ti-O, Ti-O-C, or Ti-O-Ti covalent bonds in the P-CQDs/TNRs structure (Hui et al., 2022). Even more important, increasing the intensity of absorption peaks by adding the amount of P-CQDs to TNRs is successfully introduced into the TiO2 nanorod matrix, indicating that FTIR data are consistent with the analysis of XPS results.

The various functional groups on the surface of P-CQDs have been detailed and quantified using XPS spectroscopy. XPS survey spectrum in Fig. 10 demonstrates that P-CQDs were composed of four major peaks at binding energy of 282.8, 398.9, 456.6, 528.4 eV associated with C 1 s, N 1 s, Ti 2p3/2, and O 1 s, respectively. In further analysis of the survey spectrum, the atomic composition of the P-CQDs has occurred of C, N, Ti, and O elements with the atomic percentages of 11.5, 2.6, 24.6, and 62.3 %, in turn. The deconvoluted C 1 s XPS spectra show two major peaks at 287.5 and 282.1 eV, which could be expressed presence of sp3 and sp2 of C-N-/CO and C-C/CC groups. N 1 s exhibited an intense characteristic signal at 397.3 eV due to the –N-(CO)- functional group. The high-resolution XPS spectrum for O 1 s displays distinct characteristic binding peaks energy at 529.3, 532.6, and 527.4 eV corresponding to CO, C-O, and Ti (IV) oxide. These results further support the FTIR results confirming the presence of functional groups in P-CQDs.

Survey XPS spectrum of P-CQDs/TNRs ( Fig. 11) proves the presence of C, O, and Ti elements, which is in good agreement with EDX spectroscopy. When deconvoluted the XPS spectrum, the signals of titanium (Ti 2p) were split into four primary parts with two major and two minor peaks. The core level Ti 2p spectrum, as shown in Fig. 11d, exhibits the two major characteristic binding energies of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 appeared at 457.8 eV and 463.1 eV, respectively. Also, two different surface plasmon interactions are observed by group oscillations of free electrons at the surface that two minor peaks at 470.7 eV and 476.6 eV is to assigned surface plasmons belonging to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 (Jaeger and Patscheider, 2012) fore pristine TNRs (graph not shown), peaks at 457.7 eV and 464.3 eV corresponded to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, resp. After decoration of P-CQDs, it was observed that the binding energies of major peaks have comparatively shifted, which could be indicated that P-CQDs were incorporated into its TNRs matrix resulting in the reduction of Ti3+ in P-CQDs/TNRs composites (Farjadfar et al., 2022; Liang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2021).

FE-SEM/EDX images of pure TNRs samples and P-CQDs/TNRs composites are presented in Fig. 12. According to figures (a) and (b), not only TNRs but P-CQDs/TNRs present identical rod-like morphology, and also both of them agglomerate into larger particles. Beyond that, there are differences from the point of elemental compositions between them even though the surface properties of TNRs and P-CQDs nanocomposites do not considerably exhibit visible changes.

EDX data in Fig. 12b confirmed the presence of the C element referring to the existence of P-CQDs. Upon incorporating P-CQDs into the TNRs networks, it is observed that the weight % of Ti and O content in the TiO2 sample is reduced, which implies these elements are efficiently replaced with C elements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic Removal and Inactivation of MB, Cr (VI), e-Coli and A. niger
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic potentials of as-prepared TNRs and P-CQDs/TNRs photocatalysts were investigated by studying not only the removal of MB and Cr (VI) solutions but also photokilling of e-Coli and A. niger the corresponding results are presented in
Figs. 13–16. In the photocatalytic performance evaluation, TNRs and prepared P-CQDs/TNRs-X with different volumes of carbon quantum dot were treated in the dark for 30 min for adsorption-desorption equilibrium of the pollutants on the sample surface prior to switch on irradiation source. All parameters in the removal of all pollutants are specified in Sections 2.5 and 2.6. In order to investigate the photocatalytic efficiency characterized TNRs and P-CQDs/TNRs composites for MB and Cr (VI) species, ıt was benefitted from all P-CQDs/TNRs-X nanomaterials. However, it would rather P-CQDs/TNRs-5 composite, which is the corresponding product presents the best photocatalytic removal of MB and Cr (VI). Moreover, this composite was evaluated for all characterization steps.

For MB degradation, it was found that synthesized TNRs nanoparticles under similar circumstances adsorbed 32.97 % of MB dyes and obtained 60.28 % photocatalytic efficiency after 150 min by UV-A light, while commercial TiO2 degraded 16.54 % of MB in 30 min of irradiation period and this rate reached maximum 47.64 % end of the illumination (Fig. 13a). In contrast, it can be clearly seen that the P-CQDs/TNRs-X nanocomposites show much higher degraded MB (70.87 %) under visible light irradiation. As the P-CQDs volume incorporated into TNRs skeleton increases from 1 to 25 mL, the optimal degradation rate of P-CQDs/TNRs-(1, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25) follows the sequence of 70.87, 85.80, 78.61, 84.09, 81.37, and 81.40 %, indicating that the introduction of P-CQDs molecules exhibits favoring effect on enhancing of photocatalytic performance of TNRs nanoparticle (Fig. 13b). However, it is worthy mentioning that whole nanocomposites with different P-CQDs content demonstrated much closer photocatalytic capacity for MB solution. This surprising scene may be ascribed that higher CQDs content can inhibit the active pores on the TNRs or cause the aggregation of the CQDs (Tong et al., 2022; Shen et al., 2022).

As for the removal of Cr (VI), it was shown from Fig. 14a that it removed over time with irradiation and was reduced to approximately 14.64 % of Cr (VI) to Cr (III) in the 90 min of irradiation while there was no significant change in the initial concentration of hexavalent chromium in the first 60 min in the presence of commercial TiO2. As irradiation time progresses, no difference in removal was observed, since the photocatalyst surface reached maximum saturation by the Cr (VI) ions. Yet, TNRs adsorbed Cr (VI) species at a rate of 14.16 % without irradiation and this value reached 51.46 % associated with irradiation. The reduction efficiencies of Cr (VI) by P-CQDs/TNRs composites prepared by impregnation of TNRs into solutions with different volumes of P-CQDs (1–25 mL) are illustrated in Fig. 14b. Here, the value of the sample solution was adjusted to be ∼1.5. Because the Cr(VI) species are present in the form of
Cr
2
O
7
2 −
and
HCrO
4
−
at the range of pH 1 and 2 (Acharya et al., 2018). In our experiment,
pH
pzc
of P-CQDs/TNRs is about 7.12 detected in the 10−2 M KCl solution in the range of pH: 3–11 (fixed HCl/NaOH). When the pH value of chromium solution is kept lower or equal to
pH
pzc
in photocatalytic reduction of Cr (VI), the surface of P-CQDs/TNRs nanocomposites is positively charged, and thus it is expected that the electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged Cr (VI) ions and the positively charged P-CQDs/TNRs will be facilitated. Contrary to that of lower or equal to
pH
pzc
, as pH rated up to a higher value or 7.12, the surface of P-CQDs/TNRs become negatively charged, which could obstruct the deposit the of the Cr (VI) ions on the catalysts, and leading to the efficiency of photocatalytic reduction due to blocked the electrostatic interaction between pollutant species and catalyst (Li et al., 2018). Fig. 14b displays the comparison of photocatalytic reduction performances of P-CQDs/TNRs-X nanocomposites toward Cr(VI) solution at pH 1.5 under UV-A light irradiation. After 150 min irradiation, it is seen that no significant alteration of the concentration of Cr(VI). As for photocatalytic reduction performance of nanocomposites is in order of P-CQDs/TNRs-20 > P-CQDs/TNRs-25 > P-CQDs/TNRs-5 > P-CQDs/TNRs-10 > P-CQDs/TNRs-15 > P-CQDs/TNRs-1> TNRs> commercial TiO2 (85.05, 82.49, 82.31, 80,27, 79.29, 72.61, 51.46, 19.92 %) that these values obtained end of the 150 min illumination. Besides, the photoreduction rate of Cr(VI) by P-CQDs/TNRs-20 under UV-A light is faster than that of other composites. In terms of acquired experimental data, it is very clear that containing P-CQDs in TNRs gives rise to intensified photocatalytic reduction of Cr (VI) in the aqueous medium. However, photoreduction data obtained close to each other by P-CQDs/TNRs-X containing different volumes of P-CQDs could be explained by the fact that the P-CQDs is not homogeneously distributed into the TNRs network or the low concentration of the amount of CQDs in different volumes. It is important to note that although EDTA is used to prevent swiftly returning to the valance band of transferred electrons as a result of electron transfer occurs very quickly on the TNRs samples (Rahmat et al., 2019), it can be seen that from Fig. 14b, there is still significant changes among dissimilar composites.

It was performed the photokilling capacity of the TNRs and P-CQDs/TNRs-5 against both e. coli and A. niger to bring out the activity of CQDs in improving the photocatalysis antibacterial activity of catalysts, as shown in Figs. 15–16. TNRs and P-CQDs/TNRs-5 photocatalysts showed antibacterial effect UV-A, and both samples achieved 100 % antibacterial effect on E. coli after 150 min of light exposure. But, when compared to TNRs catalysts, P-CQDs/TNRs-5 composites provided higher photokilling activity at 60 min and following. Since carbon dot molecules are electron donors, they may have transferred electrons to TNRs as co-catalysts and thus contributed to the antibacterial process (Zeng et al., 2017). Moreover, P-CQDs molecules could be enhanced the formation of reactive oxygen species (such as
HO
.
,
H
2
O
2
,
O
2
− .
) on the channels in the TNRs, inhibiting the proliferation of the E. coli by denaturing cellular components in the way of breaking the cell membrane (Zhang et al., 2021).

Fig. 16 shows a comparison of inactivation performances of both as-prepared TNRs and P-CQDs/TNRs-5 composites against A. niger under UV-a light irradiation. As seen in Fig. 16 that there is no significant difference between the growth observed after 150 min of irradiation of the blank A. niger specie without a catalyst and the growth obtained in the presence of catalysts. Especially behind unexpected this situation, it has also been reported by Yu et al., 2013, that it is thought to be resistant to heat, light, and chemical methods of fungal spores, and to have the complexity of structure as well as the thickness of their cell membranes, causing low sensitivity to photocatalytic disinfection (Yu et al., 2013). Similarly, it has been emphasized that the viability of fungal spores can continue even after switching off the irradiation source and also the propagation of fungi can make a new start although it can be prevented by photocatalytic system based on UV light in studies conducted by Chen et al., 2009a and 2009 b (Chen et al., 2009; Chen et al., 2009). It is crucial to indicate that the chitin layer appearing fungal species has higher endurance to
HO
.
radicals than glucans and peptidoglycan existing microorganisms such as gram-negative E. coli (Tzeng et al., 2022). In conclusion, it can be concluded from photokilling research that the prevention of reproduction of A. niger with as-prepared TNRs and P-CQDs/TNRs-5 nanocomposites were much more difficult than that of e-coli cells.

In this study, the eco-friendly P-CQDs as a carbon source were successfully incorporated surface of TNRs network so as to prepare a novel composite photocatalyst P-CQDs/TNRs. The P-CQDs were prepared by fix-bed pyrolysis from potato peels without ant additives, afterward, dot molecules were immobilized via the chemical impregnation method in an ultrasonic bath into the as-synthesized TNRs with the hydrothermal route. Photocatalytic properties of these new composites were investigated for MB, Cr (VI), E. coli, and A. niger as pollutants, and compared with commercial TiO2 and TNR as reference. It was found that when the P-CQDs content is 5 V % of total volume, the photoremoval activity of P-CQDs/TNRs −5 composite among all composites was highest, up to the 85.80 %, 82.31 % for MB, Cr (VI) respectively compared to the original TNRs and TiO2 nanopowder. Besides, results showed that whereas it caused to significant damage the cell permeability of E. coli microorganisms, resulting in the inactivation of the bacterial cells, P-CQDs/TNRs-5 photocatalyst displayed no photo-inhibition effect against A. niger pathogen after 150 min of irradiation. The data of this paper indicate that the fabrication of P-CQDs/TNRs-X composites decorated carbon dot molecules could contribute to the photoremoval activity by way of extending visible light absorption and boosting the transfer of photo excited charge carriers over the surface towards different pollutants in environmental applications.

Mahmut Deniz Yilmaz: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Data curation, Conceptualization. Melek Koç Keşi̇r: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization.
